```{python}
_annual_from_bills = bat_seasonal["annual_kwh"]
_max_pct_diff = float((((_annual_from_seasonal - _annual_from_bills).abs() / _annual_from_bills.abs().clip(lower_bound=1.0)).max()))
print(f"Max % diff between seasonal sum and bill-derived annual kWh: {_max_pct_diff:.4%}")

_ht_col = "postprocess_group.heating_type_v2"
_hp_mask = bat_seasonal[_ht_col].is_in([k for k, v in HT_V2_LABELS.items() if v == "Heat pump"])

_w = bat_seasonal["weight"]
W_all = float((_w * bat_seasonal["winter_kwh"]).sum())
S_all = float((_w * bat_seasonal["summer_kwh"]).sum())
W_hp = float((_w.filter(_hp_mask) * bat_seasonal.filter(_hp_mask)["winter_kwh"]).sum())
S_hp = float((_w.filter(_hp_mask) * bat_seasonal.filter(_hp_mask)["summer_kwh"]).sum())

print(f"\nWeighted seasonal kWh (all customers): winter {W_all/1e6:,.1f} GWh, summer {S_all/1e6:,.1f} GWh")
print(f"Weighted seasonal kWh (HP customers):  winter {W_hp/1e6:,.1f} GWh, summer {S_hp/1e6:,.1f} GWh")
print(f"HP winter share: {W_hp/W_all:.1%}, HP summer share: {S_hp/S_all:.1%}")

_all_row = cos_tbl.filter(pl.col("subclass") == "All customers")
_hp_row = cos_tbl.filter(pl.col("subclass") == "Heat pump")
n_all = float(_all_row["n_customers"][0])
n_hp = float(_hp_row["n_customers"][0])
C_hp = float(_hp_row["cost_of_service"][0])

F_all = _annual_fixed_per_customer * n_all
F_hp = _annual_fixed_per_customer * n_hp
R = TOTAL_DELIVERY_REVENUE_REQUIREMENT

A = np.array([[W_all, S_all], [W_hp, S_hp]])
b = np.array([R - F_all, C_hp - F_hp])
r_w, r_s = np.linalg.solve(A, b)

_sc_feasible = r_w > 0 and r_s > 0
print(f"\n--- Unconstrained solution ---")
print(f"Winter rate: {r_w * 100:.2f} ¢/kWh")
print(f"Summer rate: {r_s * 100:.2f} ¢/kWh")
print(f"Feasible (both positive): {_sc_feasible}")

if _sc_feasible:
    _sc_hp_xs_remaining = 0.0
    _sc_r_w_final = r_w
    _sc_r_s_final = r_s
    print(f"\nHP cross-subsidy eliminated exactly.")
else:
    from scipy.optimize import minimize_scalar

    def _hp_xs_at_rw(rw: float) -> float:
        rs = (R - F_all - rw * W_all) / S_all
        if rs < 0:
            return float("inf")
        hp_rev = rw * W_hp + rs * S_hp + F_hp
        return abs(hp_rev - C_hp)

    _rw_max = (R - F_all) / W_all
    _res = minimize_scalar(_hp_xs_at_rw, bounds=(0.0, _rw_max), method="bounded")
    _sc_r_w_final = _res.x
    _sc_r_s_final = (R - F_all - _sc_r_w_final * W_all) / S_all
    _hp_rev_constrained = _sc_r_w_final * W_hp + _sc_r_s_final * S_hp + F_hp
    _sc_hp_xs_remaining = _hp_rev_constrained - C_hp
    print(f"\n--- Constrained solution (both rates >= 0, revenue-neutral) ---")
    print(f"Winter rate: {_sc_r_w_final * 100:.2f} ¢/kWh")
    print(f"Summer rate: {_sc_r_s_final * 100:.2f} ¢/kWh")
    print(f"Remaining HP cross-subsidy: ${_sc_hp_xs_remaining:,.0f}/year")
    print(f"  (vs current HP cross-subsidy: ${float(_hp_row['cross_subsidy'][0]):,.0f}/year)")

_winter_summer_ratio = _sc_r_w_final / _sc_r_s_final if _sc_r_s_final > 0 else float("inf")
print(f"\nWinter/summer rate ratio: {_winter_summer_ratio:.2f}")

report_vars["sc_winter_rate"] = _sc_r_w_final
report_vars["sc_summer_rate"] = _sc_r_s_final
report_vars["sc_hp_cross_subsidy_remaining"] = _sc_hp_xs_remaining
report_vars["sc_feasible"] = _sc_feasible
report_vars["sc_W_all_gwh"] = W_all / 1e6
report_vars["sc_S_all_gwh"] = S_all / 1e6
report_vars["sc_W_hp_gwh"] = W_hp / 1e6
report_vars["sc_S_hp_gwh"] = S_hp / 1e6
report_vars["sc_winter_summer_ratio"] = _winter_summer_ratio
```

### When does a feasible seasonal solution exist?

The 2×2 system has a closed-form solution via Cramer's rule. Defining three HP consumption shares:

- $s^{\text{hp}} = K_{\text{hp}} / K_{\text{all}}$ — HP's share of **annual** kWh
- $s_w^{\text{hp}} = W_{\text{hp}} / W_{\text{all}}$ — HP's share of **winter** kWh
- $s_s^{\text{hp}} = S_{\text{hp}} / S_{\text{all}}$ — HP's share of **summer** kWh

and an HP volumetric cost-of-service share:

- $c^{\text{hp}} = v_{\text{hp}} / V$ where $v_{\text{hp}} = C_{\text{hp}} - F_{\text{hp}}$ and $V = R - F_{\text{all}}$

The flat-rate cross-subsidy is positive (HP overpays) iff $s^{\text{hp}} > c^{\text{hp}}$: HP consumes a larger share of total kWh than their share of volumetric cost-of-service.

A feasible seasonal solution (both rates non-negative) exists iff HP's cost share falls *between* their seasonal consumption shares:

$$s_s^{\text{hp}} \;\leq\; c^{\text{hp}} \;\leq\; s_w^{\text{hp}}$$

Since HP customers are winter-heavy ($s_w^{\text{hp}} > s_s^{\text{hp}}$), the binding constraint is the **summer** side: the solution fails when $s_s^{\text{hp}} > c^{\text{hp}}$ — when HP customers' share of summer consumption alone already exceeds their share of volumetric cost-of-service. In that case, even setting the winter rate to zero can't make HP customers break even, because they still overpay through their summer consumption.

When the solution is infeasible and we set $r_w = 0$ (the constrained optimum), the remaining cross-subsidy as a fraction of the flat-rate cross-subsidy has a clean expression:

$$\frac{XS_{\text{seasonal}}}{XS_{\text{flat}}} = \frac{s_s^{\text{hp}} - c^{\text{hp}}}{s^{\text{hp}} - c^{\text{hp}}}$$

**Why does this fail in RI?** The binding constraint is that HP buildings consume too much electricity in summer relative to their cost-of-service — even though heat pumps aren't heating. Per-customer summer consumption tells the story: HP buildings average ~4,600 kWh in summer vs. ~3,300 kWh for non-HP buildings — about 40% more. The most likely explanation is that heat pumps *are* air conditioners: an HP building is guaranteed to have cooling capability and is probably using it, whereas not all non-HP buildings have AC. So even in the off-peak season, HP buildings punch above their weight in consumption.

Seasonal pricing can only redistribute revenue *between* seasons. It cannot fix a within-season consumption imbalance. In RI, the seasonal approach gets 95% of the way there — the last 5% is baked into HP buildings' non-heating consumption profile.

**Generalizability across states:** Whether a seasonal rate can eliminate the HP cross-subsidy depends on the gap between $s_s^{\text{hp}}$ and $c^{\text{hp}}$: HP's share of summer kWh vs. their share of volumetric cost-of-service. In RI, the gap is tiny (0.1 pp), so a jurisdiction where HP buildings' summer consumption share is slightly lower — or HP cost-of-service share is slightly higher — would cross the feasibility threshold. But the relationship depends on building stock characteristics (AC penetration, home size) and the utility's cost allocation, not just climate. We would need to run the same calculation with each state's data to know.

```{python}
#| label: seasonal-feasibility-condition

V = R - F_all
v_hp = C_hp - F_hp
K_hp = W_hp + S_hp
K_all = W_all + S_all

s_hp_annual = K_hp / K_all
s_hp_winter = W_hp / W_all
s_hp_summer = S_hp / S_all
c_hp = v_hp / V

print("=== HP consumption shares and cost share ===")
print(f"HP share of annual kWh:  {s_hp_annual:.3%}")
print(f"HP share of winter kWh:  {s_hp_winter:.3%}")
print(f"HP share of summer kWh:  {s_hp_summer:.3%}")
print(f"HP volumetric COS share: {c_hp:.3%}")
print()

_feasibility_gap = s_hp_summer - c_hp
print(f"Feasibility gap (s_summer - c_hp): {_feasibility_gap:.3%}")
if _feasibility_gap <= 0:
    print("  → Feasible: HP summer share ≤ HP cost share")
else:
    print("  → Infeasible: HP summer share exceeds HP cost share")
    _xs_frac = _feasibility_gap / (s_hp_annual - c_hp)
    print(f"  → Remaining cross-subsidy = {_xs_frac:.1%} of flat-rate cross-subsidy")
    _xs_remaining_formula = V * s_hp_summer - v_hp
    print(f"  → Analytical remaining XS: ${_xs_remaining_formula:,.0f} "
          f"(vs numerical: ${_sc_hp_xs_remaining:,.0f})")

_n_nonhp = n_all - n_hp
_hp_summer_per_cust = S_hp / n_hp
_nonhp_summer_per_cust = (S_all - S_hp) / _n_nonhp
print(f"\n=== Per-customer summer consumption ===")
print(f"HP buildings:     {_hp_summer_per_cust:,.0f} kWh/customer")
print(f"Non-HP buildings: {_nonhp_summer_per_cust:,.0f} kWh/customer")
print(f"HP premium:       {_hp_summer_per_cust / _nonhp_summer_per_cust - 1:.0%}")

report_vars["sc_s_hp_annual"] = s_hp_annual
report_vars["sc_s_hp_winter"] = s_hp_winter
report_vars["sc_s_hp_summer"] = s_hp_summer
report_vars["sc_c_hp"] = c_hp
report_vars["sc_feasibility_gap"] = _feasibility_gap
report_vars["sc_xs_frac_of_flat"] = _sc_hp_xs_remaining / float(_hp_row["cross_subsidy"][0])
```

```{python}
#| label: bat-pipeline-hp-flat

bat_hp_flat = run_scenario(
    PATH_MASTER_BAT_1718,
    "run 17+18 (HP flat / non-HP recalibrated)",
    hp_flat=True,
)
cos_tbl_hp_flat = build_cos_table(bat_hp_flat)

bat
```

## Schema and bill identity (delivery)

Printing the schema documents what we loaded. CAIRO’s delivery identity should tie out for **both** scenarios; we already asserted in the pipeline cell.

```{python}
#| label: show-schema

print("run 1+2 schema:\n", bat.schema)
print("max |bill identity| run 1+2:", end=" ")
_check = bat.with_columns(
    (
        pl.col("economic_burden_delivery")
        + pl.col("residual_share_epmc_delivery")
        + pl.col("BAT_epmc_delivery")
        - pl.col("annual_bill_delivery")
    )
    .abs()
    .alias("e"),
)
print(float(_check["e"].max()))

print("run 17+18 schema:\n", bat_hp_flat.schema)
_check2 = bat_hp_flat.with_columns(
    (
        pl.col("economic_burden_delivery")
        + pl.col("residual_share_epmc_delivery")
        + pl.col("BAT_epmc_delivery")
        - pl.col("annual_bill_delivery")
    )
    .abs()
    .alias("e"),
)
print("max |bill identity| run 17+18:", float(_check2["e"].max()))
```

## Aggregate by subclass (reference)

We aggregate each metric with **Σ (weight × value)**. A **spine** join keeps empty subclasses and row order. **`n_customers_display`** uses largest remainder so the five subclasses sum to **`DISPLAY_CUSTOMER_TOTAL`**; **`pct_customers_display`** matches those integers. (Logic lives in `build_cos_table` above.)

```{python}
#| label: aggregate-preview

from IPython.display import display

print("run 1+2 aggregated:")
display(cos_tbl)
print("run 17+18 aggregated:")
display(cos_tbl_hp_flat)
```

## Great Tables — default rates (run 1+2)

We format **`cos_tbl`** for publication: **Customers** uses **`n_customers_display`**; **% of customers** uses **`pct_customers_display`**. HTML goes to `cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass.html` for `htmltools::includeHTML()` in `index.qmd` (avoid `{{< embed >}}` for GT). **Titles** for every table in this notebook are set in the next cell (`_GT_TITLE_COS_TOTALS` and `_GT_TITLE_COS_AVG`). Default-rate subtitles use **`_COS_SUBTITLE_VOL_DEFAULT`**: all-in calibrated volumetric rate plus PRB-1 distribution vs transmission from **`rie_rate_case_test_year.yaml`** (budget ÷ `test_year_residential_kwh`). **`subtitle=`** is set per table in the cells below (omit it or pass `None` where the builder allows, to hide the subtitle).

```{python}
#| label: cos-subclass-gt-titles

# Edit these two strings to change titles for all four Great Tables (totals vs averages).
_GT_TITLE_COS_TOTALS = (
    "Delivery revenue, cost of service, and cross-subsidy by residential subclass (9/1/24 to 8/31/25)"
)
_GT_TITLE_COS_AVG = (
    "Average annual delivery bill, cost of service, and cross-subsidy by residential subclass (9/1/24 to 8/31/25)"
)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cos-by-subclass

_cos_gt = build_gt_cos_totals(
    cos_tbl,
    title=_GT_TITLE_COS_TOTALS,
    subtitle=(
        f"All customers on default residential rates (A-16 and A-60).\n"
        f"{_COS_SUBTITLE_VOL_DEFAULT}"
    ),
)

Path("../cache").mkdir(exist_ok=True)
Path("../cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass.html").write_text(_cos_gt.as_raw_html())
_cos_gt
```

## Per-customer averages — default rates (run 1+2)

Same logic as before: averages divide by true Σ `weight` per subclass; **Avg bill alignment ÷ Avg COS** matches subclass total cross-subsidy ÷ subclass total COS.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cos-by-subclass-avg

_cos_avg_gt = build_gt_cos_avg(
    cos_tbl,
    title=_GT_TITLE_COS_AVG,
    subtitle=(
        f"All customers on default residential delivery rates (A-16 and A-60). "
        f"{_COS_SUBTITLE_VOL_DEFAULT}"
    ),
)

Path("../cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass_avg.html").write_text(_cos_avg_gt.as_raw_html())
_cos_avg_gt
```

## Great Tables — today vs proposed delivery rates

The **totals** table below compares **today's** uniform residential delivery pricing to a **proposed** split: a lower volumetric charge for heat-pump homes and a separately calibrated charge for everyone else. The **¢/kWh** figures in the column spanners match the volumetric delivery charges in the calibrated tariff inputs used for the bill model. The **per-customer averages** table in the next cell mirrors that layout: first spanner — **avg. delivery bill** and **avg. cross-subsidy** under default rates (no customer counts); second spanner — **change in avg. delivery bill**, then proposed **avg. delivery bill**, **avg. cost of service**, **avg. cross-subsidy**, and **avg. cross-subsidy ÷ avg. COS**. Cache: `cost_of_service_by_subclass_hp_flat.html` and `cost_of_service_by_subclass_avg_hp_flat.html`.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cos-by-subclass-hp-flat

_cos_gt_hp_flat = build_gt_cos_totals_hp_flat_vs_current(
    cos_tbl,
    cos_tbl_hp_flat,
    subtitle="Proposed heat-pump delivery rate compared with today's uniform rates.",
    label_spanner_today=_COS_HP_COMPARE_SPAN_TODAY,
    label_spanner_proposed_delivery=_COS_HP_COMPARE_SPAN_PROPOSED,
    label_spanner_cos=_COS_HP_COMPARE_SPAN_COS,
    title=_GT_TITLE_COS_TOTALS,
)

Path("../cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass_hp_flat.html").write_text(
    _cos_gt_hp_flat.as_raw_html(),
)
_cos_gt_hp_flat
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cos-by-subclass-avg-hp-flat

_cos_avg_gt_hp_flat = build_gt_cos_avg_hp_flat_vs_current(
    cos_tbl,
    cos_tbl_hp_flat,
    subtitle="Per-customer averages: default uniform rates vs proposed split delivery rates.",
    label_spanner_default=_COS_HP_AVG_COMPARE_SPAN_DEFAULT,
    label_spanner_proposed=_COS_HP_AVG_COMPARE_SPAN_PROPOSED,
    title=_GT_TITLE_COS_AVG,
)

Path("../cache/cost_of_service_by_subclass_avg_hp_flat.html").write_text(
    _cos_avg_gt_hp_flat.as_raw_html(),
)
_cos_avg_gt_hp_flat
```

## Export report variables

The main RI report may not need these yet, but we mirror the pattern from `analysis.qmd`: serialize `report_vars` so inline or narrative code can load a single pickle without re-running the heavy cells.

```{python}
#| label: export-report-vars

import pickle

Path("../cache/report_variables_cos_subclass.pkl").write_bytes(
    pickle.dumps(report_vars),
)

```
